hero.subtitle: Knowledge Ark is a global initiative building an ultra-durable, multi-format, and decentralised library of human achievement designed to last millennia.

pillars.title: Three Pillars of Preservation
pillars.knowledge_holders_desc: National libraries, internet archives, museums, digital repositories — the institutions that curate and safeguard humanity's intellectual heritage.
pillars.technology_drivers_desc: Ceramic media, 5D crystals, and other ultra-durable formats designed to outlast civilisations — no power required, immune to radiation and electromagnetic pulses.
pillars.infrastructure_nodes_desc: Lunar data centres, deep mountain bunkers, salt mine vaults — geographically and physically distributed storage locations on Earth and beyond.

how_it_works.title: How It Works
how_it_works.step1_desc: We map and connect knowledge holders, technology developers, and infrastructure providers worldwide.
how_it_works.step2_desc: Institutional experts select the most important records — science, culture, art, language, history.
how_it_works.step3_desc: Selected knowledge is written onto ultra-durable media: ceramic, crystal, and digital formats.
how_it_works.step4_desc: Copies are placed in maximally dispersed, protected locations — underground vaults, mountain bunkers, the Moon.

formula.title: The Formula
formula.equation: Content  +  Permanent Storage Media  +  Preservation Infrastructure
formula.desc: Multi-redundancy across media types, geographic locations, and formats — analogue and digital, Earth and space.

telegram.title: Follow us on Telegram
telegram.desc: Subscribe to our channel to be the first to read all the latest news and articles.

newsletter.title: Stay Informed
newsletter.subtitle: Subscribe to receive updates on new planetary repositories, technological breakthroughs, and newly preserved collections.

footer.about: A decentralised initiative to preserve humanity's most important knowledge across multiple storage media and locations — on Earth and in space.
footer.copyright: Knowledge Ark Initiative. Non-commercial, open, decentralised.

mission_page.title: Our Mission
mission_page.subtitle: We exist to ensure that humanity's most important knowledge survives — not for decades, but for millennia.
mission_page.why_now_p1: Throughout history, civilisations have lost irreplaceable knowledge — not because they chose to, but because they assumed it was safe.
mission_page.why_now_p2: The collapse of the Bronze Age erased records of entire civilisations. The sack of Constantinople destroyed archives that can never be recovered. The rise of Christianity inadvertently erased much of the written heritage of classical antiquity. Every surviving textual witness is priceless precisely because so many did not survive.
mission_page.why_now_p3: Today, digital information is even more fragile than parchment. The average hard drive lasts 3–5 years. The average webpage survives approximately 100 days. We are generating more knowledge than any previous era — and storing it on the most ephemeral media in human history.
mission_page.what_it_is_p1: Knowledge Ark is a coordination network — a platform that connects three groups who rarely interact:
mission_page.what_it_is_li1: Knowledge Holders — libraries, archives, museums, digital repositories
mission_page.what_it_is_li2: Storage Technology Developers — creators of ultra-durable media (ceramics, crystals, and beyond)
mission_page.what_it_is_li3: Infrastructure Providers — operators of space missions, hardened bunkers, long-duration vaults
mission_page.what_it_is_p2: Together, they form the complete chain needed to preserve knowledge permanently: content + permanent media + protected placement.
mission_page.redundancy_p: No single technology, no single location, no single format is enough. The Ark is built on the principle of maximum redundancy:
mission_page.redundancy_media_desc: Ceramic, crystal, digital — each with different failure modes
mission_page.redundancy_locations_desc: Mountains, bunkers, salt mines, the Moon — maximally dispersed
mission_page.redundancy_formats_desc: Analogue and digital — readable without specific technology
mission_page.decentralised_p1: The Knowledge Ark operates as a DAO-style network. There is no CEO, no headquarters, no single controlling entity. This is intentional.
mission_page.decentralised_p2: A centralised archive has a single point of failure. A distributed network of autonomous institutions, each contributing their expertise, is far more resilient — just like the data it protects.
mission_page.achievements_p: Future civilisations will rediscover the value of Pi on their own. What they cannot rediscover is our social fabric, our art, our culture, our languages, our music. The Ark preserves not just scientific knowledge, but the full spectrum of human achievement — the things that make us us.
mission_page.not_li1: Not a startup. Not a fundraising campaign.
mission_page.not_li2: Not a "time capsule in space" marketing project.
mission_page.not_li3: Not a replacement for the Internet Archive, Arctic World Archive, or Arch Mission.
mission_page.not_li4: Not a centralised organisation with a hierarchy.
mission_page.not_p: We are a coordination layer — connecting those who have the knowledge, those who have the technology, and those who have the infrastructure.
mission_page.origin_p1: The initiative grew from a lifelong obsession with what might be called "reverse archaeology" — stamping texts onto copper plates, sealing them in glass jars with machine oil and nitrogen, and burying them in carefully chosen locations. Letters to future archaeologists.
mission_page.origin_p2: That instinct — the drive to send something forward in time — is now backed by real technology. Ceramic data carriers that last 5,000 years. Crystals that could survive billions. Data centres on the Moon. For the first time in history, we have the tools to match the ambition.

tech_page.title: Technologies
tech_page.subtitle: These are not concepts. These are real, existing technologies being developed and tested today.
tech_page.cera_li1: Femtosecond laser writing on nano-thin ceramic layers
tech_page.cera_li2: No power required in dormant state
tech_page.cera_li4: Immune to radiation and electromagnetic pulses
tech_page.cera_desc: Connected to the Memory of Mankind (MOM) project — analogue storage on ceramic tablets inside the world's oldest salt mine in the Austrian Alps, operating since 2012.
tech_page.spho_li1: 5D memory crystals — optical storage in fused quartz
tech_page.spho_li4: Theoretical capacity: entire human library on a single disc
tech_page.spho_li5: Developed at the University of Southampton
tech_page.lone_li1: Commercial data centres on the Moon
tech_page.lone_li2: Successful data storage tests on lunar surface (2024–2025)
tech_page.lone_li4: Protection from war, natural disasters, and EMP
tech_page.multi_subtitle: No single point of failure. Every piece of preserved knowledge exists in multiple copies, on multiple media, in multiple locations.
tech_page.multi_media_desc: Ceramic, crystal, digital — each immune to different types of catastrophic failure.
tech_page.multi_geo_desc: Mountains, underground vaults, salt mines on Earth. Lunar surface in space. Maximally dispersed.
tech_page.multi_format_desc: Both analogue (human-readable without technology) and digital formats, ensuring accessibility regardless of future technological capability.
tech_page.footer_p: The network is open to new storage technologies and infrastructure partners.

network_page.title: Network Members
network_page.subtitle: A growing coalition of institutions, technologists, and infrastructure providers committed to permanent knowledge preservation.
network_page.cat_knowledge_desc: National libraries, internet archives, artistic museums, video archives, and digital repositories providing curatorial expertise and access to collections.
network_page.cat_tech_desc: Creators of storage technologies designed to outlast civilisations — ceramic, crystal, and emerging formats.
network_page.cat_space_desc: Operators of space missions, hardened bunkers, salt mine vaults, and other long-duration physical storage facilities.
network_page.cat_digital_desc: Existing digital preservation projects and platforms that form the knowledge pipeline.
network_page.placeholder_desc: Member profiles will be published here as permissions are confirmed. Each organisation controls how they are represented in the directory.

participate_page.title: How to Participate
participate_page.subtitle: No financial commitment. No immediate obligations. Just a shared belief that knowledge should outlast us all.
participate_page.node_prov_desc: Curatorial expertise and access to collections. You select what matters most.
participate_page.node_rec_desc: Your collections preserved on the most durable media ever created. Visibility in an international network of preservation institutions.
participate_page.node_com_desc: None financial. Only interest and willingness to engage in dialogue.
participate_page.tech_prov_desc: Storage technology and technical expertise. Capacity allocation for encoding.
participate_page.tech_rec_desc: Direct access to the world's largest archival institutions. Real-world use cases for your technology.
participate_page.infra_for: For: Space companies, bunker operators, vault facilities
participate_page.infra_prov_desc: Physical space and logistics for long-term placement of preserved media.
participate_page.infra_rec_desc: Participation in a landmark project. PR visibility. A network of leading institutions and technology developers.
participate_page.form_desc: A simple "we are open to the idea" is exactly what we need at this stage. Every expression of interest strengthens the case for the entire network.
participate_page.form_msg: What can you offer or how would you like to participate?

faq_page.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq_page.subtitle: Answers to the questions we hear most often from institutions and partners.
faq_page.a1: The Knowledge Ark is a decentralised, non-commercial initiative coordinated by a distributed team of cultural preservation enthusiasts, researchers, and technologists. We operate as a networking platform — connecting people and institutions who care about preserving human knowledge.
faq_page.a2: No. There is no financial commitment and no immediate obligation at any stage. This is a purely non-commercial, charitable initiative.
faq_page.a3: The initiative is currently volunteer-driven and self-funded. As the network matures, we will explore appropriate models for formalisation — but participation never requires financial contribution.
faq_page.q4: What gets included in the Ark?
faq_page.a4: The selection of materials is determined by curatorial nodes — the libraries, archives, and institutions themselves. We do not dictate what to preserve; we provide the platform, technology, and coordination for those who know best what matters.
faq_page.a5: No. This is a non-commercial, charitable initiative. No one profits from participation.
faq_page.q6: How is this different from the Internet Archive, Arctic World Archive, or Arch Mission?
faq_page.a6: We are not a single archive. We are a coordination network that connects existing archives with new storage technologies and placement infrastructure. We complement, not compete with, existing preservation efforts.
faq_page.q7: Can we just express interest without committing?
faq_page.a7: Absolutely. A simple "we are open to the idea" is exactly what we need at this stage. We are building a coalition, and every expression of interest strengthens the case for technology and infrastructure partners to allocate capacity.
faq_page.contact_p: Have a question that's not listed here?

contact_page.title: Contact
contact_page.subtitle: We'd love to hear from you — whether you're an institution, a technologist, or simply curious.
contact_page.news_text: Stay updated on the initiative's progress.
